excerpt_en: 7/17. Less than 24 hours after arriving, I did a Masu Photo shoot near Sagrada Familia with Ame, an actress and model. It was my first shoot with a Spanish person, and since I only know Spanish greetings, we mainly communicated in English — no problem at all. It seems there’s nothing like Masu Photo in Spain, so the potential here feels strong. In the afternoon I visited Gaudi’s famous buildings, Casa Batlló and Casa Milà, and summarized them. The designs inspired by nature were especially interesting. After the tapas night, I switched to a budget pasta strategy with super cheap tomato sauce.
body_en:
7/17

  • Masu Photo in Barcelona

Less than 24 hours after arriving,

I did a Masu Photo shoot near Sagrada Familia!

This time,

with Ame, an actress and model.

It was my first shoot with a Spanish person,

and I only know Spanish greetings, so

basic communication was in English.

Even so, there were no issues with the shoot,

and there’s apparently nothing like this in Spain.

It feels like Masu Photo has potential here too!

  • Touring Gaudi architecture

In the afternoon, I had no specific plans,

so I went to two places famous for Gaudi architecture.

Casa Batlló

Overview:

Casa Batlló is a famous building in Barcelona, Spain, remodeled by Antoni Gaudí between 1904 and 1906. It was originally a regular building, but Gaudí transformed it into something entirely new.

Design:

Casa Batlló is known for its unique design. The façade has a wave-like shape and is decorated with mosaic tiles. The balcony and window designs look like bones, so it’s sometimes called the “House of Bones.” The roof has decorations reminiscent of a dragon’s back, embodying Gaudí’s nature-inspired style.

Features:

  • Façade: A wavy façade decorated with colorful tiles.
  • Interior space: Clever design to maximize light and airflow.
  • Rooftop: Decorations reminiscent of a dragon’s back.

Casa Batlló is a representative example of Gaudí’s creativity and innovation, and it is registered as a UNESCO World Heritage site.

Casa Milà

Overview:

Casa Milà, also known as “La Pedrera,” is an apartment building constructed between 1906 and 1912. It is located in the center of Barcelona and is one of Gaudí’s later works.

Design:

Casa Milà’s design is known for its undulating stone façade and distinctive iron balconies. The entire building is inspired by nature, with almost no straight lines. The interior structure is also unique, with a large central courtyard designed to bring natural light into all rooms.

Features:

  • Façade: A wavy stone exterior with iron balconies.
  • Interior space: A large courtyard on each floor to bring in natural light.
  • Rooftop: Gaudí’s unique chimneys and ventilation towers.

-

Casa Milà is also registered as a UNESCO World Heritage site, and symbolizes Gaudí’s genius in architecture and design philosophy.

These buildings are important heritage sites that symbolize Gaudí’s originality and Barcelona’s architectural culture.

That’s a quick summary.

*GPT helped

I personally walked around both, and

it was fun.

Gaudí’s architecture is completely different from what I’ve seen before,

and I think what I found especially interesting

is that it’s inspired by nature.

  • Cost-effective pasta first

Yesterday I enjoyed tapas, so

from here, it’s a frugal life.

So,

what I bought at the supermarket was

tomato sauce!

Only about 200 yen!

Cheap!

I can use this twice,

so it’s 100 yen per meal!

Even with pasta, it’s about 300 yen per meal.

I don’t want to spend time cooking,

so this pasta strategy is the strongest.

When I was an address hopper in Japan,

if there were no dinners or drinks with others,

I cooked for myself,

so I’m used to this life. Lol

Being able to carry it without a fridge is the best.

excerpt_en: 7/18. There are many bars and clubs in Barcelona, and promoters invite people staying at hotels/hostels to daily events. I joined a group and went to a “best view of Barcelona” event, but no one showed up, so I toured alone and worked while enjoying the view. I used a 10-ride transit ticket (€12.5). Later I visited La Boqueria Market and saw the crowds, but I didn’t eat there. Dinner was a simple McDonald’s set. At night I watched my first real flamenco at Tablao Flamenco Cordobés, a famous venue founded in 1970. I had a front-row side seat with a drink ticket (sangria). The quality was high and it was overwhelming, though I felt some prior knowledge would help.
body_en:
7/18

  • To the best views of Barcelona

Around Barcelona,

there are lots of bars and clubs,

and since events happen every day,

promoters invite people staying at hotels and hostels.

I’m in a group,

and they run some kind of event every day,

so I went to one called “Let’s see Barcelona’s best view.”

This time I used the metro and bus.

*By the way, I bought a 10-ride ticket.

It’s about €12.5, so around 2,500 yen.

A single ride is €2.55,

so the 10-ride ticket is better value.

I went to the meeting spot alone.

The meeting place was on a hill, so I climbed up,

and there were a few tourists and locals.

But I couldn’t find anyone who seemed to be there for the same event! Lol

It turned out to be zero people!!

So,

I did sightseeing alone! Lol

The view was amazing,

so I worked while looking at it.

It was the perfect workation,

but getting sunburned was unavoidable.

  • To La Boqueria Market

After that, I went to La Boqueria Market.

La Boqueria is a very famous market on La Rambla in Barcelona,

and its official name is Mercat de Sant Josep.

It has a history going back to the 13th century,

and the current market was officially established in 1840.

The arched entrance is decorated with colorful glass mosaics,

welcoming visitors.

https://www.notion.so

It was super lively.

Mostly tourists, it seemed.

Places like this are

not that fun to eat around alone,

and the prices are for tourists,

so I just looked around and left.

For dinner, I went to McDonald’s.

The cheapest set was about 700 yen.

That’s enough.

  • To Flamenco

This America + Spain trip is also about

seeing world-class entertainment.

The reason is simple: if you don’t know the best,

you can’t create entertainment that gets close to that quality.

So,

I went to authentic flamenco for the first time in my life.

This time I watched at

Tablao Flamenco Cordobés, one of the most famous in Barcelona.

Tablao Flamenco Cordobés was founded in 1970,

and is one of the most famous flamenco tablaos in Barcelona.

*By the way, a “tablao” is a place dedicated to flamenco performances. The word comes from the Spanish “tabla,” meaning “floor” or “stage.” Tablaos are small theaters, bars, or restaurant-like venues where flamenco is performed directly for audiences.

I was able to watch from the very front on the side.

I booked a ticket with one drink included,

so I got sangria when it started.

Cordobés is a very famous place,

so high-quality performers gather from all over Spain.

Honestly, I could tell the quality was high, but

I didn’t have enough knowledge to understand the fine details.

Still, it was overwhelming.

I’ve seen a lot of entertainment and art,

and honestly, without some knowledge,

it’s hard to understand “what’s amazing,”

so even a little research beforehand might help!

Anyway, flamenco was interesting!

So, day 3 in Barcelona is done!

excerpt_en: 7/29. In the morning I did a Masu Photo shoot at Sagrada Familia — my second in Barcelona. The model was Ukrainian and works as a model and in a restaurant, so I shared FOMUS with her. Afterward I got gelato (honestly just okay) and went to the Museu Nacional d’Art de Catalunya. It’s huge, with Romanesque, Gothic, Renaissance/Baroque, and modern collections — useful for thinking about FOMUS’s art display. At night I joined the hostel’s club tour. I pre-gamed with people from Argentina, Slovenia, and Korea, then went to the club. It was loud and hard to communicate, and I’m more of a quiet one-on-one person, so it was a good experience but not something I want to repeat.
body_en:
7/29

In the morning,

I did a Masu Photo shoot at Sagrada Familia!

This makes it my second one here!

She’s Ukrainian, and works as a model and in a restaurant,

so I talked about FOMUS masu with her.

It’s really fun to see

more FOMUS masu members like this,

even in Spain!

  • To the Museu Nacional d’Art de Catalunya

After the shoot, I had some free time,

so I went to eat gelato.

Honestly, it was just okay.

Then I went to the Museu Nacional d’Art de Catalunya.

The Museu Nacional d’Art de Catalunya is one of Barcelona’s major museums,

especially known for Catalan art.

It opened in 1929 and is located on Montjuic Hill

in the grand Palau Nacional.

Collection: The collection is very diverse, including:

  • Romanesque art: One of the world’s largest Romanesque collections, with 11th–13th century frescoes, sculptures, and metalwork, especially murals moved from Catalan churches.
  • Gothic art: 13th–15th century Catalan Gothic works, including sculpture, painting, and metalwork.
  • Renaissance and Baroque: Major Spanish and European Renaissance and Baroque works.
  • Modern and contemporary art: Many 19th–20th century Catalan works, including Impressionism, Modernism, and Art Nouveau.

You can also see works from the Catalan Renaissance era

and modern artists like Joan Miro and Antoni Tapies.

It was huge here too, like New York.

Looking at display methods and such

connects to FOMUS art as well,

so I’m glad I could see it.

  • To a Barcelona club

The hostel I’m staying at

runs events every day.

It’s not just for this hostel,

but a club tour for tourists in the area.

I’d been planning to go at least once,

and the next day I only had an early morning plan to shoot “Masu Camera x Sagrada Familia,”

so I went tonight!

Before that, we toasted at the hostel

with people from Argentina, Slovenia, and Korea.

In Spain, the sun doesn’t set until after 9 PM,

so dinner is late too.

Clubs really get going around 11 PM.

Off to the club.

Clubs are super loud,

so even talking is hard.

Especially in an environment where it’s hard to hear English,

communication is really tough.

And of course, no Japanese people.

European students are on summer vacation,

so it seemed even more lively.

When they learned I was Japanese,

they said things like:

“From that far away!”

“Welcome to Spain! I’ll buy you a beer!”

“I like Japan and want to go someday!”

It was fun talking.

That said,

I’m not naturally good with club environments,

and I prefer one-on-one in a quiet place,

so as an “experience,” it was good,

but I don’t want to go again.

Also, it’s hard to talk business in a club.

People are there to party, so you can’t do serious talk.

So,

I probably won’t go for no reason in the future.

Since it was a club tour,

we went to three places, and I left partway.

I forgot my earphones,

so I walked about 30 minutes back alone with loud music blasting

*It was 2 AM

*Don’t copy this

*This is Barcelona

excerpt_en: 7/20. I got back to the hotel at 2 AM, then at sunrise (7 AM) went to Sagrada Familia with the Masu Camera and my camera to capture “Masu Camera x Sagrada Familia” before the crowds. There were people, but far fewer. After shooting, I went back to the hotel and slept. I’ve been moving nonstop, so rest matters. After waking, I focused on desk work — communicating with models and FOMUS meetings. Lunch was homemade pasta with sliced ham.
body_en:
7/20

I returned to the hotel at 2 AM,

and from sunrise at 7 AM,

I went to Sagrada Familia with the Masu Camera and my camera.

It was to properly capture a “Masu Camera x Sagrada Familia” photo.

By 9 AM there are already tourists,

so I thought early morning would be less crowded.

When I actually went, there were people, but

far fewer!

So I snapped the shots.

*This is iPhone and unedited.

I got a good photo.

After shooting,

I went back to the hotel and slept. Lol

I’ve been moving constantly lately,

so rest is important too.

*Slept hard

After waking up,

I did nothing but desk work!

Communication with models,

FOMUS meetings, and so on.

Lunch was homemade pasta.

*Added sliced ham because it was 1 euro

Thanks to that, work progressed a lot.

I’m not here just to sightsee,

so I can’t be playing every day.

But this balance

feels really comfortable to me.

Alright, I’ll keep pushing.

excerpt_en: I did a morning Masu Photo shoot with Marietta, again with Sagrada Familia as the backdrop. Because she came with her boyfriend, I kept it quick but still talked about FOMUS. In the afternoon I visited Park Guell, where crowds gathered around the big lizard. It made me want to create a “giant Masu turtle” for FOMUS someday. Then I went to the gelato shop DelaCrem — third in Barcelona — and it was the best so far; I got pistachio plus hazelnut. Later I finally went inside Sagrada Familia. The tickets had been sold out, so I extended my stay by one night. The interior pillars were like trees, and I was surprised to see sea turtles and land turtles supporting the exterior columns. It made me happy since FOMUS has a turtle character.
body_en:
• Morning Masu Photo shoot

Today I had another Masu Photo shoot.

The model was Marietta.

Again, the background was Sagrada Familia.

Since it’s Barcelona,

that’s the most important part,

so we shoot here, and I stayed within a 5-minute walk for that reason.

She came with her boyfriend,

so I thought I shouldn’t drag it out,

and shot quickly!!

Of course, I talked about FOMUS too!

Taking a two-shot in front of the boyfriend...

How is that? Lol

If I were the boyfriend, I wouldn’t mind a two-shot,

but it probably depends on the person...

Still, both of them enjoyed it. ✨

- To Park Guell

In the afternoon, I went to Park Guell, which I had booked.

Park Guell features Gaudi’s unique architecture,

and was originally planned as a residential area,

but now it’s a popular tourist spot with mosaic benches and lizards.

People were gathered around the big lizard.

Someday, I want to make a “giant Masu turtle” for FOMUS

and turn it into a landmark.

#I need to get strong fast for that

Still,

both in America and Barcelona,

everyone comes with someone,

and there are no solo visitors. Lol

Was tourism always like this?

#I’ve been solo so much lately that I’ve forgotten normal tourism

  • To a gelato shop

After Park Guell, I went to a gelato shop.

Barcelona has lots of gelato shops,

but not all of them are good,

so I always check reviews on Google

and pick good-looking places.

This time was my third in Barcelona: DelaCrem.

It was a long line!!

It was Sunday, but still,

there was a huge line. Lol

It was worth it though —

it was by far the best gelato so far.

I always choose

“pistachio + something.”

This time, it was hazelnut.

That was 4.4 euros.

In today’s yen, it’s about 780 yen!

There are three types of spending:

investment, consumption, and waste.

Basically,

I only spend on investment (self-investment)

or consumption (living costs), but

since coming to Spain,

I’ve been “wasting” only on gelato.

As a sweets lover,

I can’t resist gelato...

#too weak

- Inside Sagrada Familia

Finally, Sagrada Familia.

I originally planned to leave Barcelona

by this morning, but

tickets for Sagrada Familia were sold out more than expected,

so I extended my stay by one night and finally went inside!

#By the way, I bought this ticket on the last day in New York

#As early as possible

#I underestimated reservations

Gaudi poured his artistic and architectural ideals

into this basilica,

incorporating organic designs inspired by nature,

and the columns inside are literally “trees.”

The pillars branching like tree trunks

gave me goosebumps.

It may not connect directly to FOMUS yet,

but I’m glad I got to see it in person.

And what surprised me was

the exterior columns are supported by

sea turtles and land turtles!

They were turtles! Lol

FOMUS has a turtle character,

so that made me a little happy.

That wraps up my Barcelona activities!

Tomorrow I’ll move to Madrid.

What kind of encounters await?

I’m excited already!

excerpt_en: I left Barcelona for Madrid by high-speed train. I booked only three days in advance, so it was a bit expensive — about 18,000 yen — but it took under three hours. For my six nights in Madrid, I’ll be Couchsurfing with Jose. It’s a private room, a bit messy but I’m grateful for a bed. It seems I can’t borrow a spare key, so I need to return while the owner is home, and they often go out in the evening. I rushed to the supermarket and bought water, pasta, and muffins, then cooked and ate alone. The desk work time was valuable. Madrid life starts now.
body_en:
I left Barcelona and headed to Madrid.

This time I used a high-speed train.

I booked only about three days in advance,

so it was a bit expensive — about 18,000 yen.

If I had booked earlier,

it probably would have been under 10,000 yen.

Still, I arrived in under three hours.

From there, I went to my Couchsurfing host.

Yes, all six nights in Madrid

are Couchsurfing.

It took about an hour to get there.

I’m staying at Jose’s house from today.

The room is private,

and it’s a bit messy, but

I’m just grateful to have a bed!

They were going out in about an hour,

and from what I heard, I probably can’t borrow a spare key.

That means I have to come back when the owner is home.

And the owner often goes out from evening to night,

and today they said they’re leaving at 5 PM.

It was already 4 PM.

On this day, I only moved,

so I hadn’t eaten breakfast or lunch, and I was starving.

But in one hour, I wouldn’t be able to go out.

I rushed to the nearby supermarket.

It was a 15-minute walk one way, and I shopped at full speed.

I bought the essentials: water, pasta, and muffins.

Since everyone went out,

I boiled pasta and ate alone.

In my case, when I’m alone,

I have tons of desk work,

so that time is valuable too.

Thanks to that, work progressed well.

So, life in Madrid starts now!

I’m excited!

excerpt_en: My first shoot in Madrid fell through. The model changed the meeting place the night before, I arrived at 11, and waited with no contact. After an hour I got an email saying she felt sick and couldn’t come. I decided not to work with her again because she didn’t respect the appointment. I switched plans and booked the Prado Museum. It has a massive collection of Western art, especially Spanish painting. I also discovered a great way to use ChatGPT: translate the artwork label, paste it into GPT, and ask questions. It answered why the laurel wreath appears in Poussin’s “Parnassus,” and helped me enjoy art solo more deeply.
body_en:
Today was my first shoot in Madrid!

Last night, the model offered a change of meeting place, and it was fine, so I accepted.

I arrived at 11, but

there was no contact!

I sent a photo saying “I’m here!”

Since the place was changed last night

and we confirmed the time,

it’s unlikely she just forgot.

If you don’t contact people the day before,

overseas people will easily forget.

*I learned this in the Philippines

Honestly,

waiting 10 minutes is fine.

I’m used to it.

But after 30 minutes, still nothing...

Hmm??

If an hour passed with no contact,

I planned to cancel, walk around,

and use the time by shooting Masu Camera, etc.

Then I got an email:

“I’m not feeling well and can’t go.”

“I’m sorry.”

What???

Normally you’d contact me as soon as you knew you couldn’t come, right??

Then I wouldn’t have had to wait.

That email arrived at 12.

So an hour with no contact.

And she’s someone who actually works as a model.

I won’t work with her again.

Because she can’t keep promises.

It’s fine if you’re late.

If you’re going to be 30 or 60 minutes late,

tell me in advance, and I can use that time elsewhere.

Without contact,

I can’t leave the place.

If you can’t imagine you’re causing trouble for others, that’s out.

This is just my opinion,

and I don’t expect everyone to agree,

but that’s how I am.

So,

I got a little irritated. Lol

Still, it’s a waste to stay annoyed, so I changed the schedule.

I quickly looked online and booked the Prado Museum!

The Prado Museum has a massive collection of Western art,

and is especially known for Spanish painting.

*No photos because photography wasn’t allowed

Main works:

  • Diego Velazquez: “Las Meninas,” etc.
  • Francisco Goya: “The Nude Maja,” “The Clothed Maja,” etc.
  • El Greco: “The Assumption of the Virgin,” etc.
  • Also works by Rubens, Titian, Bosch, Raphael, and more.

It was huge,

and took about 4 hours to walk around.

And I discovered something new here!

Museum x ChatGPT

GPT is high-spec, so

when you have questions about a painting,

you can read the label text with a translation tool,

and feed it to GPT.

Then it outputs information about the painting.

And if you ask, “Why is this painting like that?”

it answers.

Here’s what I actually did:

[Massu]

I’m at the Prado Museum looking at art.

I have a question about a piece.

Nicolas Poussin (1594-1665)

Parnassus

It depicts the mythological Mount Parnassus dedicated to Apollo and the Muses, celebrating art, especially poetry. In the center, the god offers the nectar of the gods to a poet crowned with laurel, probably Homer. In the foreground, two putti personifying a nude woman provide inspiring water from the Castalian spring to the poets. Inspired by Raphael’s fresco on the same subject in the Vatican, this painting is likely an homage to the Italian poet Giovanni Battista Marino (1569-1625), a patron of the young Poussin.

Why is he wearing a laurel wreath?

And GPT replied:

Wearing a laurel wreath was a symbol of victory and honor in ancient Greece and Rome. It was especially awarded to poets, scholars, and heroes in recognition of their achievements.

In Nicolas Poussin’s painting “Parnassus,” Apollo offers nectar to the poet Homer, who wears a laurel wreath. This symbolizes honoring Homer’s great achievements as a poet. The laurel is associated with Apollo, the patron god of poetry and the arts, who is also often depicted wearing a laurel wreath himself.

This painting emphasizes the importance of art and poetry, expressing respect and admiration for great poets. Therefore, Homer wears the laurel wreath as a symbolic expression to celebrate his achievements and make him a lasting figure in memory.

Isn’t that amazing?

Even without feeding the image itself,

it can answer the meaning of the laurel wreath.

With this, you can deepen your understanding of art even alone.

Of course, it’s not always correct.

But it was a new discovery.

excerpt_en: During the day I did nonstop desk work for FOMUS and AI projects. Even overseas, there are days I don’t go out and just work, though staying home all day is tough. At night I met Sandra, who messaged me via Couchsurfing. She’s coming to Tokyo for 10 days in September and wanted advice, so we went bar hopping. In Spain, bars are usually for families, friends, or couples, so this kind of opportunity is valuable for me as a solo traveler. We ended up visiting four bars and exchanged travel info. We talked about masu too — Spain doesn’t have it, but folding fans are common, so there’s room to expand.
body_en:
So,

during the day I just did desk work nonstop.

I work on FOMUS and AI projects,

so even overseas,

there are days I don’t go out and just work.

That said, staying home all day is tough. Lol

To a Madrid bar

At night, I went to a bar with Sandra,

who messaged me on Couchsurfing!

She’s coming to Tokyo for 10 days this September,

and wanted tips, so we met!

#First time meeting, of course

In both America and Spain,

nighttime bars are usually for families, friends, or couples.

There isn’t much of a culture of drinking alone.

I’m mostly solo,

so opportunities like this are really helpful.

#I can drink beer openly outside

I let Sandra choose the places and we hopped bars!

There are tons of bars within walking distance,

so it’s easy to find them.

We ended up visiting four places because the conversation got lively.

#About 4 hours

I shared recommendations from places I’ve been,

and Sandra told me about Madrid.

This kind of relationship is truly appreciated!!

We also talked about masu,

and apparently Spain doesn’t have masu.

But folding fans are popular.

In a way, it feels like there’s room to expand.

We talked about so many things,

and it was a super meaningful time!!

title_en: A Day That Reminded Me of Cultural Differences
excerpt_en: Today was intense. First, the Sagishima art gallery project in Japan had disagreements, so I joined the meeting. The questions were all about the business model, which didn’t match the purpose of a hands-on container-painting event, so I asked that person not to participate in the work. FOMUS is about building culture, not chasing small short-term money. Then came culture shock at my Couchsurfing place in Madrid: the host is a nudist, and other guests are too. They said it was uncomfortable that I wore clothes. Combined with not having a spare key and strict return times, I decided to move to a hotel. Despite all this, I still shot three Masu Photo sessions: an 18-year-old Spanish girl, a Chinese resident, and a Colombian lawyer/artist who used translation tools. I worked nonstop and ended the day with McDonald’s and gelato before more desk work.
body_en:
Today was another hard day.

Three major events.

Sagishima Project

First,

the FOMUS art gallery project in Japan.

We’re using an empty container on Sagishima in Hiroshima,

to build an art gallery

and help more people discover Sagishima.

This time, someone outside the usual FOMUS members joined,

but apparently there were disagreements,

so I joined the meeting on short notice.

It’s important for the project leader to communicate their vision,

so I explained a lot.

However, all the questions were about the business model,

and this event was meant to paint the container,

while the future direction is something FOMUS will decide.

Also, and this may sound harsh,

the person already runs an art gallery and is an artist,

so even when I explained

“a different approach from before” and

“why we’re doing it here,”

they didn’t really understand.

What FOMUS is thinking about

is long-term gallery operations,

not small short-term profits.

People who’ve spent a lot of time with me understand this,

but someone I’ve never met before, at a hands-on work event,

taking a “why do we even do this?” stance

and focusing on “business models, blah blah,”

is off from the event purpose,

so I asked them to step away from the work

to avoid causing unnecessary trouble for other participants.

FOMUS culture and thinking

is a community that can always change

by reading the flow of the times,

so people who talk from rigid, old-school ways

often reject my ideas because they don’t understand them.

That’s why people who intuitively think “this is interesting!”

gather around FOMUS.

That exchange gets even more exciting,

and makes the whole community more fun —

so people who want small short-term money

are not needed in FOMUS.

I’m building culture,

so those who can’t resonate with that,

we decline for now.

That’s how you build a strong, dense community.

Still,

I’m not denying anyone as a person.

Everyone thinks differently,

so it’s just a matter of fit.

Culture shock at my Couchsurfing place

I’m in Madrid now,

and I’m Couchsurfing at a local’s house.

I found someone who lets me stay 6 nights / 7 days,

and today was the halfway point —

then I got an email from the host.

“It’s uncomfortable that you’re wearing clothes.”

Actually,

I’m staying at a nudist house,

and there are always about 5–6 people staying.

I knew in advance that the host is nude,

and I understood that before staying.

But the other guests are nude too,

and they felt uncomfortable that I was clothed.

#behind my back, basically

In my life,

I had never once experienced

“someone feeling uncomfortable because I’m wearing clothes,”

so this was shocking.

Also, in other homes I got a spare key

and could move freely, but

here I couldn’t get a spare key,

so the host had to open the door every time,

and since they often go out at night,

there were days I had to be back by 7 PM.

It was difficult, and

being here would bother people and restrict my movement,

which is the hardest thing, so

I decided to move to a hotel starting today.

I knew it was a nudist house,

but I didn’t expect this.

If you ever stay at a nudist house,

I recommend confirming this in advance. Lol

Another unexpected story was born.

So, it was a day where I felt strong cultural differences.

Even with all of this happening,

from morning to evening,

I still did three Masu Photo shoots.

A Spanish 18-year-old girl.

https://www.notion.so

She’s coming to Japan with her mom next time,

so I plan to give her advice.

The next shoot was with a Chinese person living in Madrid.

But due to the region, she couldn’t speak Chinese.

We were close in age, so the conversation was fun!

And the last one was a Colombian woman living in Madrid.

She’s a lawyer, an artist,

and she’s starting a real estate business too.

She wasn’t good at English,

so we used translation tools to write and exchange what we wanted to say.

We were able to communicate surprisingly well,

probably because it was just the two of us.

I’m strongest one-on-one.

That was reaffirmed.

So from morning to evening,

I was running nonstop,

and didn’t even have time to eat breakfast or lunch.

After that, I moved to a hotel,

and immediately met again online with FOMUS members on Sagishima.

We shared our vision (which we already had),

and I still had desk work left,

so I quickly ate at McDonald’s,

then had gelato,

and worked nonstop — that was the day.

title_en: Ceiling Paintings at the Royal Palace
excerpt_en: Only two days left in Madrid. I had planned to go to Toledo but stayed because of yesterday’s issues and project communications. In the afternoon I went to the Royal Palace of Madrid (15 euros) with a pre-purchased ticket. I was especially interested in the ceiling paintings for inspiration for the art gallery we’re planning. The palace was stunning, and the ceiling art was top-tier among what I’ve seen abroad. I also went for churros with hot chocolate at a famous shop near the palace. The churros were average but the chocolate was great.
body_en:
Only two days left in Madrid!

So today I had no shoots.

The reason was that I planned to go to Toledo.

It’s about an hour from central Madrid,

so I wanted to explore it, but

after yesterday’s mess,

and needing to communicate about projects in Japan,

I decided not to go to Toledo.

Honestly, there will be many chances to go to Toledo in the future.

So there was no need to force it.

So,

in the afternoon, I went to the Royal Palace of Spain.

It cost about 15 euros to enter.

I had bought a ticket in advance,

so I could enter without lining up.

The Royal Palace of Madrid (Palacio Real de Madrid)

is the official residence of the Spanish royal family,

and a place for official events and state ceremonies.

History

  • Construction: The palace was built in the 18th century. After the old Alcazar burned down in 1734, construction began by order of Philip V, and Charles III first moved in in 1764.
  • Design: The initial design was by Italian architect Filippo Juvarra, later continued by Giovanni Battista Sacchetti and Francesco Sabatini.

Architecture and decoration

  • Structure: A grand white stone building, over 135,000 square meters, with more than 3,000 rooms.
  • Interior: Lavish decorations, with valuable art, furniture, and tapestries. Each room has different themes and styles, including Baroque, Rococo, and Neoclassical.

Personally, I was interested in the palace ceiling paintings.

I looked them up in advance,

and the quality is insanely high,

and since we plan to create ceiling paintings

in the art gallery we’re working on, I wanted to see them.

Using this kind of architecture

as reference for the FOMUS gallery

is one of my goals.

Seeing it in person was just incredible!!

https://www.notion.so

https://www.notion.so

https://www.notion.so

https://www.notion.so

It truly felt like a royal palace.

Among all the ceiling paintings I’ve seen abroad,

this was top-class!!

Personally, I like Japanese ceiling paintings best! Lol

Surprisingly, there were square designs with floral patterns,

so that seemed like a good reference too.

*Could be mistaken for a masu

The palace was full of discoveries!

Madrid sweets

Other than the palace, I had no plans,

so I went to try Madrid sweets — churros.

I went to a famous churros shop about 10 minutes from the palace.

https://www.notion.so

https://www.notion.so

The standard here is to dip churros in hot chocolate.

This was 1,000 yen.

Honestly, the churros taste was normal.

But the chocolate was delicious.

There were six pieces,

so sharing with two people would be just right.
